units.name: Recap: explicit-density models and GAN setup
units.description: Connects prior explicit-density material to implicit-density modeling and introduces GANs.
units.summary: The lecture opens by re-establishing the generative-model taxonomy: discriminative models learn p(y|x), unconditional generative models learn p(x), and conditional generative models learn p(x|y), with the key distinction being what the probability distribution normalizes over [ts=20s]. It reviews explicit-density methods from the prior lecture: autoregressive image models turn pixels or subpixels into a discrete sequence modeled by an RNN or Transformer [ts=100s], while VAEs jointly train an encoder q(z|x) and decoder p(x|z) to maximize a variational lower bound on data likelihood [ts=132s]. The new focus is implicit-density models, which cannot return a numerical p(x) but can sample from the learned distribution [ts=184s]. GANs are introduced as a direct implicit-density method: sample a latent z from a simple prior such as a unit Gaussian, pass it through a generator G(z), and tune the induced generator distribution pG to match pdata [ts=320s]. A discriminator D is added to classify real versus fake images, and the generator learns from discriminator gradients backpropagated through the generated image [ts=455s], [ts=512s].

key_points.text: Discriminative models learn p(y|x), generative models learn p(x), and conditional generative models learn p(x|y).
Implicit density models cannot output a density p(x) but can sample from the learned distribution.
GANs give up directly modeling p(x) but still provide a way to sample from the fitted distribution.
A latent z is sampled from a simple prior p(z), typically a unit Gaussian, then passed through G(z).
GAN training tries to make the generator-induced distribution pG match pdata.
The discriminator D classifies input images as real or fake.
The generator receives learning signal as gradients backpropagated through the discriminator and generated image.

units.name: GAN minimax objective and instability
units.description: Derives the GAN minimax game and explains why its training signal is hard to diagnose.
units.summary: The GAN objective is formalized as a minimax game between G and D [ts=553s]. With G fixed, D is trained as a binary classifier: maximize log D(x) on real data and log(1 - D(G(z))) on generated data so real samples get D(x)=1 and fake samples get D(x)=0 [ts=596s], [ts=664s]. With D fixed, the real-data term no longer depends on G, so G minimizes the fake-data term to make D classify generated samples as real [ts=720s]. Training alternates gradient ascent on D and gradient descent on G over the value V(G,D) [ts=784s], [ts=818s]. A crucial diagnostic warning is that V is not a conventional loss: its absolute value does not directly indicate sample quality or convergence because it depends on both D and G [ts=847s]. This makes GANs effective but difficult to train, tune, and diagnose, since the problem is inherently unstable and lacks a reliable decreasing loss curve [ts=917s].

key_points.text: GANs jointly train G and D with a minimax game.
For fixed G, D maximizes log D(x) on real data and log(1-D(G(z))) on generated data.
For fixed D, G cares only about the fake-sample term and tries to make D classify fake samples as real.
Training alternates gradient ascent for D and gradient descent for G on V(G,D).
The minimax value V is not an overall loss, so its absolute value is not a reliable training-quality metric.
GAN objectives are unstable because the same quantity is maximized and minimized by different networks.

units.name: GAN practical fixes, theory, architectures, and limitations
units.description: Covers non-saturating GAN training, theoretical optimum, major GAN architectures, latent interpolation, and practical limitations.
units.summary: The instructor analyzes early GAN training dynamics: a randomly initialized generator outputs obvious noise, so the discriminator quickly learns to reject generated samples, placing the generator in a flat-gradient region of the original log(1 - D(G(z))) objective [ts=968s], [ts=1026s]. A practical non-saturating trick is introduced: train G to minimize -log D(G(z)) instead, which gives stronger generator gradients at the start of training [ts=1282s], [ts=1290s]. The theoretical justification is sketched: for any fixed pG, the inner discriminator optimum has D*_G(x)=pdata(x)/(pdata(x)+pG(x)), and the outer problem is minimized when pG=pdata, though finite-capacity networks and finite-data optimization give no convergence guarantee [ts=1341s], [ts=1392s]. Architecturally, the lecture highlights DC-GAN as the first non-toy GAN architecture and StyleGAN as a more modern design with adaptive normalization [ts=1471s], [ts=1519s]. GAN latent interpolation is shown as evidence that generator latent spaces can be smooth [ts=1538s], but the lecturer warns that mode collapse or memorizing a few training samples would not yield such smoothness [ts=1684s]. The segment closes by contrasting GANs with VAEs: GANs can produce crisp, high-quality images, but are unstable and hard to diagnose, whereas VAEs have explicit encoders but tend to produce blurry samples [ts=1600s], [ts=1804s].

key_points.text: At early training, a random generator emits obvious noise and the discriminator quickly solves real-vs-fake classification.
The original generator objective has nearly flat gradients when D(G(z)) is close to 0.
The common non-saturating trick trains G to minimize -log D(G(z)) for stronger early gradients.
For fixed pG, the optimal discriminator is D*_G(x)=pdata(x)/(pdata(x)+pG(x)).
The ideal outer objective is minimized when pG(x)=pdata(x), but finite networks and data give no convergence guarantee.
DC-GAN was highlighted as the first GAN architecture with non-trivial, non-toy image results.
StyleGAN uses a more complex architecture with adaptive normalization.
GAN latent vectors can be interpolated so generated images change smoothly, but collapse or memorization can break this.

units.name: Diffusion intuition and the move to rectified flow
units.description: Introduces diffusion as iterative denoising and sets up rectified flow as the lecture's clean diffusion formalism.
units.summary: The lecture transitions to diffusion models, warning that the literature has many competing mathematical formalisms and notation choices, while this lecture will use the cleaner rectified-flow view [ts=1855s], [ts=1900s]. The core intuition is that diffusion, like GANs, tries to convert samples from a simple noise distribution into samples from pdata, but instead of learning a one-shot deterministic map G(z), it learns an iterative denoising procedure [ts=1919s], [ts=1935s]. The noise variable must have the same shape as the data, and noisy samples are described by a noise level t where t=0 is clean data and t=1 is full noise [ts=1946s], [ts=1978s]. A neural network is trained to take a noisy sample xt and t and remove a small amount of noise [ts=2013s]. At inference time, sampling starts from pure noise and applies the network repeatedly until a clean-looking generated sample emerges [ts=2046s], [ts=2101s]. The segment ends by narrowing from the general diffusion picture to rectified flow, treated here as a diffusion-style model [ts=2155s].

key_points.text: Diffusion literature has many mathematical formalisms, so the lecture uses rectified flow as a clean implementation.
Diffusion converts noise samples into data samples using iterative denoising rather than a one-shot generator.
The noise distribution must have the same shape as the data.
Noise level t ranges from 0 for clean data to 1 for full noise.
A denoising network receives xt and t and learns to remove a small amount of noise.
Inference starts from pure noise and repeatedly applies the denoiser to reach a clean sample.

units.name: Rectified flow training and sampling
units.description: Gives the concrete rectified-flow training and sampling algorithms.
units.summary: Rectified flow is presented as a concrete, simple diffusion implementation with a noise distribution pnoise and a data distribution pdata [ts=2216s]. Each training step samples z from the simple prior, x from the dataset, and t uniformly from [0,1] [ts=2232s], [ts=2251s]. It then defines a straight-line interpolation xt between x and z and a velocity vector v=z-x from the clean data point toward the noise sample [ts=2262s], [ts=2277s]. The neural network f_theta(xt,t) is trained to predict that velocity using mean squared error [ts=2306s], [ts=2358s]. The lecturer emphasizes that, unlike GANs, diffusion/rectified-flow training gives a meaningful loss curve that usually decreases as the model improves [ts=2367s]. For sampling, one chooses a fixed step count, often T=50, samples pure noise, then marches backward from t=1 to t=0 [ts=2431s], [ts=2445s]. At each step the model predicts vt and the sample is updated by stepping against the predicted noise direction, gradually removing noise until x0 is obtained [ts=2471s], [ts=2542s].

key_points.text: Rectified flow uses a simple pnoise and the target pdata.
Each training iteration samples z~pnoise, x~pdata, and t~Uniform(0,1).
Rectified flow sets xt as a linear interpolation between x and z and v=z-x.
The network f_theta(xt,t) is trained to predict the velocity vector v.
The training loss is mean squared error between the ground-truth velocity and model prediction.
Diffusion training regains a meaningful loss curve compared with GAN training.
Sampling usually chooses a fixed number of steps, with T=50 a common starting point.
During sampling, the model predicts vt at each noise level and updates the sample by stepping against that vector.

units.name: Conditional diffusion, CFG, and noise schedules
units.description: Explains conditional rectified flow, classifier-free guidance, and why non-uniform noise schedules are used.
units.summary: Conditional rectified flow extends the dataset from x to pairs (x,y) and gives y as an auxiliary model input, so sampling can be controlled by a user-provided condition such as text or an image [ts=2730s], [ts=2759s]. Because naïve conditional models may not obey the conditioning strongly enough, classifier-free guidance (CFG) modifies training by randomly dropping the condition, often about 50% of the time [ts=2806s], [ts=2823s]. This forces a single model to learn both unconditional velocity vectors v_empty toward p(x) and conditional velocity vectors v_y toward p(x|y) [ts=2864s], [ts=2881s]. During sampling, CFG forms v_cfg=(1+w)v_y - w v_empty so larger w overemphasizes the conditioning signal [ts=2904s], [ts=2937s]. The cost is that each sampling step usually evaluates the model twice, doubling inference cost [ts=2950s]. The lecture then discusses noise schedules: uniform sampling over t gives equal emphasis to easy endpoints and hard middle-noise cases [ts=3008s], [ts=3053s]. Practical schedules such as logit-normal sampling put more weight in the middle, and shifted schedules help high-resolution data where pixel correlations change how much noise is needed [ts=3090s], [ts=3113s].

key_points.text: Conditional rectified flow trains on pairs (x,y) and feeds y as an auxiliary input.
Classifier-free guidance randomly drops the conditioning signal during training.
Dropping the condition forces the model to learn an unconditional velocity vector toward p(x).
Keeping the condition gives a conditional velocity vector toward p(x|y).
CFG uses v_cfg=(1+w)v_y - w v_empty to emphasize conditioning.
CFG typically doubles sampling cost because the model is evaluated twice per step.
Uniform t sampling gives equal weight to easy endpoints and hard middle-noise cases.
Logit-normal and shifted noise schedules put more training emphasis where denoising is harder or resolution-dependent.

units.name: Latent diffusion and diffusion transformers for text-to-image
units.description: Shows how practical image generation uses latent diffusion, VAE/GAN autoencoders, and diffusion transformers.
units.summary: Latent diffusion is introduced as the dominant practical form of diffusion: first train an encoder-decoder to map high-resolution images into a smaller latent space, often downsampling spatially so the diffusion model operates on a cheaper representation [ts=3154s], [ts=3162s]. The diffusion model is then trained on noisy latents rather than raw pixels, with the encoder frozen while latents are extracted [ts=3195s], [ts=3222s]. At inference time, the model denoises a random latent repeatedly, then the decoder converts the clean latent back into an image [ts=3232s]. The encoder-decoder is typically a VAE with a very small KL prior weight, but VAE decoders can be blurry, so modern LDM pipelines add a GAN-style discriminator to improve perceptual sharpness [ts=3266s], [ts=3304s]. This is why modern pipelines combine VAE, GAN, and diffusion components [ts=3335s]. The lecture then introduces diffusion transformers (DiTs), standard Transformer-block diffusion models whose key architectural issue is how to inject timestep and conditioning information [ts=3357s], [ts=3373s]. Timestep conditioning is commonly inserted via predicted scale/shift, while text or other conditioning is inserted through cross-attention or joint attention [ts=3397s], [ts=3424s]. Text-to-image systems pass a prompt through a frozen text encoder such as T5 or CLIP, combine text embeddings with noisy latents in a diffusion transformer, and decode clean latents into an image [ts=3469s], [ts=3493s].

key_points.text: Latent diffusion first trains an encoder-decoder that maps images to a lower-dimensional latent space.
The diffusion model is trained on noisy latents, with the encoder frozen during diffusion training.
At inference, a random latent is denoised repeatedly and decoded into an image.
The latent encoder-decoder is typically trained like a VAE with a small KL prior weight.
A GAN-style discriminator is added because plain VAE decoders tend to produce blurry outputs.
Modern latent diffusion pipelines combine VAE, GAN, and diffusion components.
Diffusion transformers use standard Transformer blocks; the core architectural question is conditioning injection.
Text-to-image latent diffusion uses frozen text encoders such as T5 or CLIP to condition a diffusion transformer.

units.name: Text-to-video diffusion and distillation
units.description: Extends latent diffusion to video, explains scaling bottlenecks, and introduces distillation for faster sampling.
units.summary: Text-to-video diffusion keeps the same broad latent diffusion pipeline as text-to-image, but the latent tensor gains a temporal dimension in addition to height and width [ts=3530s], [ts=3544s]. The decoder becomes a spatial-temporal autoencoder that upsamples latents into video pixels [ts=3555s]. The major bottleneck is sequence length: high-resolution, high-FPS, many-frame video produces far more tokens than images [ts=3580s]. The lecture contrasts roughly 1,024 image tokens in a state-of-the-art text-to-image model with about 76,000 video tokens in the discussed text-to-video example [ts=3592s], [ts=3609s]. It frames 2024-2025 as a rapid era of video diffusion models, with Sora cited as an influential scaling moment for video diffusion transformers [ts=3623s], [ts=3661s]. The instructor also mentions very recent video generation examples that jointly model audio with video [ts=3703s], [ts=3730s]. The segment ends with diffusion distillation: because sampling may require 30-50 or more model evaluations, distillation attempts to reduce inference steps while preserving sample quality, sometimes even reaching single-step sampling at a quality cost [ts=3750s], [ts=3783s].

key_points.text: Text-to-video diffusion adds a temporal dimension to the latent tensor.
Video decoders are spatial-temporal autoencoders that upsample latent videos into pixels.
Video diffusion is expensive because high-resolution, high-frame-rate video creates very long token sequences.
The example contrasts about 1,024 image tokens with about 76,000 video tokens.
Sora is described as a major scaling moment for video diffusion transformers.
Diffusion sampling is slow because it requires many iterative model evaluations.
Distillation reduces the number of inference steps while trying to preserve sample quality.
Some distillation methods reach single-step sampling but usually trade off generation quality.

units.name: Generalized diffusion perspectives and autoregressive latents
units.description: Surveys generalized diffusion choices, mathematical interpretations, and the return of autoregressive models on latents.
units.summary: The final technical segment broadens from rectified flow to a generalized diffusion design space [ts=3841s]. Rectified flow is restated as sampling x, z, and t, setting xt as a linear interpolation, setting the ground-truth velocity vgt=z-x, predicting v from f_theta(xt,t), and minimizing an L2 loss [ts=3864s], [ts=3894s]. Other diffusion formulations vary the t distribution, the functions used to mix x and z into xt, and the target ygt that the network predicts [ts=3916s], [ts=3946s]. Examples include variance-preserving schedules, variance-exploding schedules, x-prediction, noise-prediction, and v-prediction [ts=3990s], [ts=4026s]. The lecturer then names three mathematical perspectives without deriving them: diffusion as a latent variable model similar to a VAE optimizing a variational lower bound [ts=4074s], diffusion as score-function learning where the score is the gradient of log pdata(x) pointing toward high-density regions [ts=4117s], and diffusion as solving stochastic differential equations with learned numerical integrators [ts=4176s]. The lecture closes by noting that autoregressive models also return in modern pipelines when trained over discrete latents from an encoder-decoder, so GANs, VAEs, autoregressive models, and diffusion all combine in contemporary generative systems [ts=4265s], [ts=4298s].

key_points.text: Rectified flow is one simple case where xt is linear interpolation and the target velocity is z-x.
Generalized diffusion varies p(t), the noisy-sample construction xt, and the target ygt.
Variance-preserving formulations choose mixing weights so unit-variance independent x and z keep unit variance.
Variance-exploding formulations set a(t)=1 and b(t)=sigma(t).
Targets may be x-prediction, noise-prediction, or v-prediction.
Diffusion can be viewed as a latent variable model optimized with a variational lower bound, similar to VAEs.
Diffusion can be viewed as learning the score function, the gradient of log p(x) pointing toward high-density regions.
Diffusion can be viewed as solving stochastic differential equations with learned numerical integrators.
